of 1 29
The Origin of a Second May Be Profoundly Deeper Than We Previously
Thought
Ian Beardsley
March 20, 2026!
of 2 29
Introduction to the Trilogy
This collection of three essays presents a line of inquiry that began with a seemingly simple
observation: the number one second appears as a characteristic time in physical systems
spanning from subatomic particles to the Solar System. What started as an investigation into
the proton radius and a proposed normal force with gradually expanded
into a quantum‑analog description of planetary orbits, and ultimately led to an unexpected
convergence with ancient metrology, sacred numbers, and the possibility of a forgotten
advanced civilization.#
The three papers are intended to be read as a progression, yet each is written to stand on its
own. Together they form a single narrative: a physical constant—or at least a persistent
invariant—may be encoded not only in the structure of matter and the architecture of our Solar
System, but also in the units of time that ancient cultures deliberately chose.#
Paper 1: Proposal for a Universal Particle Equation#
The ﬁrst paper introduces the concept of a normal force with second,
which is argued to be Lorentz invariant. From this, a master equation is derived that relates the
mass and radius of the proton, neutron, and electron, yielding predictions that align closely
with experimental values. The appearance of the golden ratio and Fibonacci approximations
ties these results to a structural recurrence relation proposed by Kristin Tynski, suggesting that
such scaling may be a fundamental feature of nature.#
Paper 2: A Quantum Analog for the Solar System #
#
The second paper extends the 1‑second theme to the Earth‑Moon‑Sun system. By taking the
ratio of the kinetic energies of the Moon and Earth, the 24‑hour day is mapped to one second.
A “planetary Planck constant” is introduced, and a Bohr‑like quantization yields the kinetic
energy of the Earth with 99.5 % accuracy. The number 108 emerges from Vedic cosmology and
leads to a general equation for the radius of a habitable planet that holds across
main‑sequence stars, suggesting that Earth’s size may be optimal for life.#
Paper 3: The Origin of a Second May Be Profoundly Deeper Than We Previously Thought #
#
The third paper turns to archaeology and the history of timekeeping. Following the work of
Flavio Barbiero, it is shown that the modern second (86 400 per day) can be understood as a
reﬁnement of a more natural unit obtained by dividing the mean solar day into 80 000 parts,
yielding seconds. This unit and its multiple 108 recur in ancient sacred architecture,
calendars, and the Vedic relations examined in Paper 2. The paper then discusses Graham
Hancock’s evidence for a lost Ice Age civilization that may have deliberately encoded such
knowledge. The convergence of physical theory, metrology, and symbolic archaeology raises
the possibility that this lost civilization possessed an understanding of universal planetary
scaling—insights that we are only now rediscovering.#
The three papers together argue that the second, as we use it today, may be more than a
human convention. It could be the vestige of an ancient, sophisticated science that recognized
a fundamental timescale woven into the fabric of matter, the dynamics of planetary systems,
and perhaps even the conditions for life elsewhere in the cosmos. Whether this knowledge
originated from a forgotten terrestrial civilization or from sources beyond our planet, the trail of
numbers—1, 1.08, 108, 80 000—invites us to reconsider the depth of human history and the
universal principles that may have guided it. — Deep Seek!

A Proposal For A Universal Particle Equation
Ian Beardsley
March 10, 2026
ABSTRACT
A Normal force where , which we suggest is Lorentz invariant, is
introduced that determines a master equation for the proton, neutron, and electron, a kind of
Universal Particle Equation. It is suggested that when we push on a particle we rotate some of its
temporal velocity into spacial velocity and resistance to this rotation is experienced as the normal
force pushing back creating inertia that we experience as mass.
The Proposal
We can form two equations where the proton radius to its mass produces about 1-second [3, 4]:

is exposed to the normal force, mediated by the 'stiffness of space' as measured by ,
producing the proton mass, .

it as (by equating them) [3, 4]:

Discussion
It is thought that the proton does not have an exact radius, but that it is a fuzzy cloud of
subatomic particles. As such depending on what is going on can determine its state, or effective
radius. It may be these different sizes are predicted by Fibonacci approximations to [4]. If
such an approximation is given by it could be that the proton radius is as large as
Which it was nearly measured to be before 2010 in two separate experiments. One using
hydrogen spectroscopy, the other electron scattering. In 2010 The recommended CODATA value
was . Then came the shocking 2010 measurement that was 4.2%
smaller using the new Muonic hydrogen result, which was . This resulted in the
famous “proton radius puzzle”.
We might suggest that the proton radius might get still smaller, closer to something using the
Fibonacci approximation of . In which case we would have:

Quantum Analog For The Solar System
Ian Beardsley
March 7, 2026
ABSTRACT
We find if consider the evolved state of the Solar System, that its quantum analog to the Bohr
atom is based on a characteristic time of one-second and the Earth's Moon as the defining metric.
1.0 The Quantum Solution To The Solar System
The ancient Sumerians (4500 BCE-1900 BCE) used base 60 counting, and divided the Earth day
into 24 hours. The ancient Egyptians (3100 BCE-30 BCE) divided the Earth day into 24 hours as
well. Since they both divided the day into 12 hours, and the night into 12 hours and, in the
winter, the night is longer than the day and in the summer, the day is longer than the night, the
hours in a day, or night, can be longer or shorter depending on the time of the year. The ancient
Greeks took the 24 hour day from the ancient Egyptians (Hipparchus, 190 BCE-120 BCE) and
and used an hour to be represented by the equinoxes when day equals night, inventing the
equinoctial hour. It was Christiaan Huygens (1629-1695) who took the hour that had been
divided up into 60 minutes, with each minute divided into 60 seconds, from the ancient Sumerian
base 60 counting, and built the first pendulum clock that could measure down to the second
accurately. This was fueled by the need of Newton's (1642-1727) world view for gravity and
mechanics that needed to measure time down to a unit as small as a second.
It is an interesting phenomenon that the Moon near perfectly eclipse the Sun. The eclipse ratio
that allow for this is about 400:
where is the radius of the Sun and is the radius of the Moon. is the orbit radius of the
Earth orbit and is the orbital radius of the Moon. The solar radius is about 400 times the lunar
radius; the Earth-Sun distance is about 400 times the Earth-Moon distance.
The number of seconds in a day are given approximately by:
The number of seconds in a day, 86 400, can be factored as:
The factor 400 is the eclipse ratio. The factor (216) relates to sixfold symmetry, hexagonal
tiling, and the approximation used by Archimedes as his starting point for calculating .
The appearance of 86 400 in ancient timekeeping thus incorporates the eclipse ratio, whether by
accident or by design.
Let us suggest that the kinetic energy of the Moon to the kinetic energy of the Earth maps the 24
hour (Earth rotation period) day into 1 second, our basis unit of measuring time:

Using average orbital velocities. We can get closer to a second using aphelions and perihelions

The Moon stabilizes Earth's axial tilt:
The Moon stabilizing the Earth's tilt to its orbit prevents extreme hot and cold on Earth and
allows for the seasons. As such the Moon is key to optimizing conditions for life on the planet.
Perhaps making it possible for intelligent life to evolve.

possible 1-second invariant that may exist across vast scales from atoms to the Solar System. We
have

constant of gravity). The product of (the charge of an electron squared) and (the mass of an
electron) become a mass cubed. We will choose the mass of the Moon, . We have the ground
state equation is:

Where we have converted meters to seconds by measuring distance in terms of time with the
speed of light ( ). We see the mass of the Moon maps the kinetic energy of the Earth over one
second to 1 second. The Moon is the metric.
The solution for the orbit of the Earth around Sun with the Schrödinger wave equation can be
inferred from the solution for an electron around a proton in the a hydrogen atom with the
Schrödinger wave equation. The Schrödinger wave equation is, in spherical coordinates
Its solution for the atom is as guessed by Niels Bohr before the wave equation existed:
is the energy for an electron orbiting protons and is the orbital shell for an electron with
protons, the orbital number. I find the solution for the Earth around the Sun utilizes the Moon
around the Earth. This is different than with the atom because planets and moons are not all the
same size and mass like electrons and protons are, and they don't jump from orbit to orbit like
electrons do. I find that for the Earth around the Sun
is the energy of the Earth, and is the planet's orbit. is the radius of the Sun, is the
radius of the Moon's orbit, is the mass of the Earth, is the mass of the Moon, is the orbit
number of the Earth which is 3 and is the Planck constant for the solar system. Instead of
having protons, we have the radius of the Sun normalized by the radius of the Moon.
We see that the Moon is indeed the metric, as we said before.
The kinetic energy of the Earth is (using orbital velocity at perihelion):

The kinetic energy of the Earth is about equal to the energy of the system, because the orbit of
the Earth is nearly circular. That is
The whole object of developing a theory for the way planetary systems form is that they meet the
following criterion: They predict the Titius-Bode rule for the distribution of the planets; the
distribution gives the planetary orbital periods from Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation. The
distribution of the planets is chiefly predicted by three factors: The inward forces of gravity from
the parent star, the outward pressure gradient from the stellar production of radiation, and the
outward inertial forces as a cloud collapses into a flat disc around the central star. These forces
separate the flat disc into rings, agglomerations of material, each ring from which a different
planet forms at its central distance from the star. In a theory of planetary formation from a
primordial disc, it should predict the Titius-Bode rule for the distribution of planets today, which
was the distribution of the rings from which the planets formed.
Also, the Earth has been in the habitable zone since 4 billion years ago when it was at 0.9 AU.
Today it is at 1AU, and that habitable zone can continue to 1.2 AU. So we can speak of the
distance to the Earth over much time. The Earth and Sun formed about 4.6 billion years ago. As
the Sun very slowly loses mass over millions of years as it burns fuel doing fusion, the Earth
slips minimally further out in its orbit over long periods of time. The Earth orbit increases by
about 0.015 meters per year. The Sun only loses 0.00007% of its mass annually. The Earth is at
1AU=1.496E11m. We have 0.015m/1.496E11m/AU=1.00267E-13AU. So,
The Earth will only move out one ten thousandth of an AU in a billion years. Anatomically
modern humans have only been around for about three hundred thousand years. Civilization
began only about six thousand years ago.
The Moon slows the Earth rotation and this in turn expands the Moon's orbit, so it is getting
larger, the Earth loses energy to the Moon. The Earth day gets longer by 0.0067 hours per million
years, and the Moon's orbit gets 3.78 cm larger per year.
We suggest the Solar system comes into phase with a possible one second invariant when the
Earth-Sun separation, and Earth-Moon separation, have kinetic energies whose ratio maps the 24
hour day into the 1-second base unit as given by equation 4:
That is is when equations 5 and 10 hold:

of 12 29
Something remains to be done. Is there something about the Sun that is common to other types of
stars; stars that are perhaps larger and hotter than the Sun, or perhaps smaller and cooler, or a
different color, like blue or red, instead of yellow? The answer is yes. I actually found something
in ancient Vedic knowledge, in the Hindu traditions. Apparently, in Hindu yoga the number 108
is an important number. I read that yogis today noticed that the diameter of the Sun is about 108
times the diameter of the Earth and that the average distance from the Sun to the Earth is about
108 solar diameters, with 108 being a significant number in yoga. So I wrote the equivalent:
or for any star and habitable planet:
the radius of the star. the orbital radius of the habitable planet. We consider the HR
diagram that plots temperature versus luminosity of stars. We see the O, B, A stars are the more
luminous stars, which is because they are bigger and more massive and the the F, G stars are
medium luminosity, mass, and size (radius). Our Sun is a G star, particularly G2V, the two
because the spectral classes are divided up in to 10 sizes, V for five meaning main sequence, that
it is part of the S shaped curve and is in the phase where the star is burning hydrogen fuel, its
original fuel, not the by products. And the K and M stars are the coolest, least massive, least
luminous.
Let us consider the habitable zones of different kinds of stars. In order to get , the
distance of the habitable planet from the star, we use the inverse square law for luminosity of the
star. If the Earth is in the habitable zone, and if the star is one hundred times brighter than the
Sun, then by the inverse square law the distance to the habitable zone of the planet is 10 times
that of what the Earth is from the Sun. Thus we have in astronomical units the habitable zone of
a star is given by:
the luminosity of the star, the luminosity of the Sun. AU the average Earth-Sun separation,
which is 1. The surprising result I found was, after applying equation 4, hypothetically predicting
the size of a habitable planet, to the stars of all spectral types from F through K, with their
different radii and luminosities (the luminosities determine , the distances to the
habitable zones), that the radius of the planet always came out about the same, about the radius
of the Earth. This may suggest optimally habitable planets are not just a function of their distance
from the star, which is a big factor in determining their temperature, but are functions of their
size and mass meaning the size of the Earth could be good for life chemistry and atmospheric

composition, and gravity. Stars of the same particular luminosities, temperatures and colors have

spectral class M stars and consistency got this result. It may be this radius for a planet is optimal
for life, in particular intelligent life, because given we might, for that, need a material

3.0 Jupiter and Saturn
We want to find what the wave equation solutions are for Jupiter and Saturn because they
significantly carry the majority of the mass of the solar system and thus should embody most
clearly the dynamics of the wave solution to the Solar System. We also show here how well the
solution for the Earth works, which is 99.5% accuracy.
I find that as we cross the asteroid belt leaving behind the terrestrial planets, which are solid, and
go to the gas giants and ice giants, the atomic number is no longer squared and the square root of
the orbital number moves from the numerator to the denominator. I believe this is because the
solar system here should be modeled in two parts, just as it is in theories of solar system
formation because there is a force other than just gravity of the Sun at work, which is the
radiation pressure of the Sun, which is what separates it into two parts, the terrestrial planets on
this side of the asteroid belt and the gas giants on the other side of the asteroid belt. The effect
the radiation pressure has is to blow the lighter elements out beyond the asteroid belt when the
solar system forms, which are gases such as hydrogen and helium, while the heavier elements are
too heavy to be blown out from the inside of the asteroid belt, allowing for the formation of the
terrestrial planets Venus, Earth, and Mars. The result is that our equation has the atomic number
of the heavier metals such as calcium for the Earth, while the equation for the gas giants has the
atomic numbers of the gasses. We write for these planets
So, for Jupiter we have (And again using the maximum orbital velocity which is at perihelion):
Jupiter is mostly composed of hydrogen gas, and secondly helium gas, so it is appropriate that
.
Our equation for Jupiter is
Where is the atomic number of hydrogen which is 1 proton, and for the orbital
number of Jupiter, $n=5$. Now we move on to Saturn...

The Origin of a Second May Be Profoundly Deeper Than We Previously
Thought
Ian Beardsley#
March 19, 2026!
of 21 29
Introduction
The author’s work shows a possible 1-second invariant across scales from the atom to the
Solar System. #
A Normal force where , which we suggest is Lorentz invariant, is
introduced that determines a master equation for the proton, neutron, and electron, a kind of
Universal Particle Equation. It is suggested that when we push on a particle we rotate some of its
temporal velocity into spacial velocity and resistance to this rotation is experienced as the normal
force pushing back creating inertia that we experience as mass. [1]
We find if consider the evolved state of the Solar System, that its quantum analog to the Bohr
atom is based on a characteristic time of one-second and the Earth's Moon as the defining metric.
[1]
With the realization that the second has ancient origins, the search for a deeper meaning leads
to a consideration of ancient history and archaeology where its development is concerned.
After much research into its development, the author ﬁnally encountered a strong lead to
something deep concerning the second when he encountered the work of Flavio Barbiero.#
The Discovery of Flavio Barbiero
The hour was ﬁrst invented in ancient Egypt by dividing the night and day into 24 units, 12 for
the day and 12 for the night. Since the day is longer in the summer, and the night shorter, and
in the winter the is day is shorter and the night is longer the length of an hour depends on the
season. The ancient Greek astronomer, Hipparchus, divided the day and night into hours
determined by the length of day and night during spring and fall equinoxes when length of day
equals the length of night, inventing the equinoctial hour used year round. Hipparchus had
access to ancient Babylonian knowledge of celestial motions where they knew the day of 24
hours gave an hour that could be divided by 60 minutes, and each minute by 60 seconds. The
Babylonians got the base 60 divisions of the hour from the ancient Sumerians. But passage of
time wasn't measured down to the second until Christiaan Huygens invented his pendulum
clock, which was demanded by the astronomical revolution that came about from the work of
Copernicus (Earth moves around the Sun), Galileo (Earth is not at the center of the Universe
from looking at Jupiter's moons with his telescope), Brahe (data for planetary motions), Kepler
(explains Brahe's data introducing elliptical orbits for the planets), and Newton (explains
Kepler's laws of planetary motion with his universal law of gravitation). [1]#
The mean solar day is 86,400 seconds. Due to gradual slowing of Earth’s rotation, it is today
86,400.002 seconds, but the 86,400 seconds is used for standard civil engineering.#
If we are to divide the mean solar day to make another fundamental unit other than the second,
Flavio Barbiero suggested in his paper The Unit of Time and the Mesoamerican Calendar [2]
we would look at dividing by 80,000 instead of the 86,400 because he suggests that was what
was originally done by an ancient, lost civilization. He suggests they were technological
because in order to do this, they had to have reasons for it based on knowledge that would
require advanced astronomy. He further says our second derived from 86,400 has its origin in
the 80,000 factor, as we will presently explain. He suggests the 80,000 division of the mean
solar day provides for the optimal calendar, that nothing can be used to do better.#
F
n
= h /(ct
2
1
)
t
1
= 1second
of 22 29
He calls the unit of time that follows from dividing-up the Earth’s rotation period with 80,000,
the universal unit of time, U. It is in seconds:#
#
The integer multiple of this, ten, is 108. As you may know, in my work I formulated an equation
for the Earth’s radius based on the ancient Vedic sacred number, 108 [1]. Apparently, in Hindu
yoga and Vedic knowledge the number 108 is an important number. I read that yogis today
noticed that the diameter of the Sun is about 108 times the diameter of the Earth and that the
average distance from the Sun to the Earth is about 108 solar diameters, with 108 being a
signiﬁcant number in yoga. So I wrote the equivalent:#
#
or for any star and habitable planet:#
#
the radius of the Earth, the radius of the Sun, the orbital radius of the Earth,
the radius of the planet, the radius of the star, and the orbital radius of the
habitable planet. We consider the HR diagram that plots temperature versus luminosity of
stars. We have the O, B, A stars are the more luminous stars, which is because they are bigger
and more massive and the the F, G stars are medium luminosity, mass, and size (radius). Our
Sun is a G star, particularly G2V, the two because the spectral classes are divided up in to 10
sizes, V for ﬁve meaning main sequence, that it is part of the S shaped curve and is in the
phase where the star is burning hydrogen fuel, its original fuel, not the by products. And the K
and M stars are the coolest, least massive, least luminous.#
Let us consider the habitable zones of diﬀerent kinds of stars. In order to get , the
distance of the habitable planet from the star, we use the inverse square law for luminosity of
the star. If the Earth is in the habitable zone, and if the star is one hundred times brighter than
the Sun, then by the inverse square law the distance to the habitable zone of the planet is 10
times that of what the Earth is from the Sun. Thus we have in astronomical units the habitable
zone of a star is given by:#
#
the luminosity of the star, the luminosity of the Sun. AU the average Earth-Sun
separation, which is 1. The surprising result I found was, after applying equation 2,
hypothetically predicting the size of a habitable planet, to the stars of all spectral types from F
through K, with their diﬀerent radii and luminosities (the luminosities determine , the
distances to the habitable zones), that the radius of the planet always came out about the
same, about the radius of the Earth. This may suggest optimally habitable planets are not just a
function of their distance from the star, which is a big factor in determining their temperature,
but are functions of their size and mass meaning the size of the Earth could be good for life

of 23 29
chemistry and atmospheric composition, and gravity. Stars of the same particular luminosities,
temperatures and colors have about the same mass and size (radius).#
Flavio Barbiero explains the perfect calendar thusly;#
We want a calendar that reconciles the tropical year (365.2422 days) with the mean solar day
(86400 seconds). Currently, we use the Julian year (365.25 days). Which means every 4 years
we have to add one day if a year is to have 365 days. The Julian year is 0.0078 days longer
than the actual length of the year:#
(365.25)(24)(60)(60)=31, 557, 600 seconds#
365.25-365.2422=0.0078#
After 1/0.0078=128.205 years ~ 128 years we have an excess of 1 day. We would add 1 day
every 4 years except after 128 years. Thus, there exists a cycle of 128 years, currently, to
correct our calendar. The 128 year cycle is:#
(128 years)(365.2422 days/year)=4,675.0016 days#
#
It has an error of #
#
Flavio Barbiero says “This fraction is the natural candidate to become our unit of time.”
Because, #
#
He continues, …”we would have a full day in excess only after 625 x 128 = 80,000 years.#
“In theory, then, if we add one day at the end of the 625th period of 128 years, we could have a
cycle of 80,000 years with a zero average error per year. It is impossible to imagine a more
precise calendar.”#
Barbiero concludes that the second we have today could have come from this because while it
is a round ﬁgure convenient for the division of the day into equal parts — 10 hours of 8,000
units each, and subdivisions of 400, and 20 units, with 8,000 equal to 20 cubed giving a year in
months of 20 — it is only divisible by 2 and 5, a number divisible by also by 3 would be more
ﬂexible and useful. He says this could be achieved easily by dividing the natural unit U by 1.08.
That is, 80,000 x 1.08 equals the 86,400 we use today that is evenly divisible by the odd
number 3, not to mention 2, 4, 5, 6, 10, 12, 15, and 30 as well.. That we would deﬁne a new
unit of time by 1/1.08 of the natural unit of time, U, where U=(0.0000125)(86,400)=1.08
seconds. That is the new unit of time would be:#
1
0.0016
= 0.0000125 =
1
80,000
days
year
1
0.0016
= 625
of 24 29
#
Barbiero goes on to tell us numbers like 80,000, 86,400, and 1.08 and their multiples and
submultiples are highly recurrent throughout the whole ancient world in terms of astronomical
knowledge and mythological numerology. For example, he tells us in sacred architecture, like
the ancient temple of Angkor Vat in Cambodia, it has 54 towers, and 108 statues along the
entrance (54 a multiple of 108), and there are 540 eﬃgies of the gods Deva and Asura (a
multiple of 108), and that the temple in Baalbek had 54 columns. He goes on in great depth in
his paper explaining this kind of archaeology.#
But we want to mention as well that the 1.08 seconds is close to one second, and in the
author’s theories for the atom and the solar system, characteristic times can vary around 1-
second from about 0.995 seconds to 1.1 seconds to 1.2 seconds [1]. For example the author o
writes [1]:#
Let us suggest that the kinetic energy of the Moon to the kinetic energy of the Earth maps the 24 hour
(Earth rotation period) day into 1 second, our basis unit of measuring time:
Where is the inclination of the Earth to its orbit.
Using average orbital velocities. We can get closer to a second using aphelions and perihelions and
perigees and apogees.
The incredible thing is Barbiero may have found extraordinary evidence in favor of
Graham Hancock’s theory of an ancient pre-ﬂood lost, advanced civilization that
disseminated knowledge to primitive cultures thousands of years ago. Barbiero writes:#
We can conclude with a high degree of confidence that the actual unit of time, the
second, is the result of a deliberate choice made by a civilization able to calculate with
great precision the length of the tropic year. An unknown civilization from which all the
ancient civilizations have been originated, or at least influenced. Clear evidence of this is
provided by mythological figures and sacred numbers left by the latter all over the world.

of 25 29
Hancock’s Core Argument (The "Magicians of the Gods" Thesis)#
Hancock proposes that a highly advanced, global "mother culture"#
existed at the end of the last Ice Age (circa 12,800 to 11,600 years#
ago). This civilization possessed sophisticated knowledge of#
astronomy, architecture, and agriculture. It was largely destroyed by#
a cataclysmic event (often linked to the Younger Dryas period,#
possibly a comet impact/airburst).#
The survivors of this catastrophe, "the Magicians" or "the Sages,"#
dispersed around the world, bringing their knowledge, symbols, and#
architectural styles to the emerging "cradle" civilizations we know—#
Mesopotamia, Egypt, the Indus Valley, and Mesoamerica. They acted as#
civilizing inﬂuences, kick-starting these cultures.#
The "Handbag of the Gods" Motif#
A Sumerian God with the Hand bag#
An Olmec Depiction of someone
with the handbag#
At the top of this Pillar at Gobekli Tepe, 6000 years before the Sumerians,
we see the same handbag.#
This is one of Hancock's most visually compelling pieces of evidence.#
The "handbag" (or bucket) symbol appears in:#
of 26 29
Göbekli Tepe: Carved on pillars in Turkey, dated to 10,000 BCE.#
Mesopotamia: Held by winged genies or deities like Apkallu sages in#
Assyrian reliefs.#
Mesoamerica: Held by gods like Quetzalcoatl in Olmec and later Aztec#
art.#
Elsewhere:Similar forms appear in ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs and#
even in distant cultures like the Maori of New Zealand.#
Hancock's Interpretation: This cannot be coincidence. It is a shared#
symbolic relic of the original lost civilization, representing sacred#
knowledge or the "gifts of civilization" given by the survivor-sages.#
Its persistence across millennia and continents is a clue to a common#
source.#
The Role of the Green Sahara#
This is a crucial geographical pivot in his theory.#
The Green Sahara Period: From roughly 10,000 to 5,000 years ago, what#
is now the Sahara desert was a vast savanna with lakes, rivers, and#
abundant life. It supported human populations.#
The Desertiﬁcation: Around 5,000 years ago, climate change rapidly#
desertiﬁed the region, forcing populations to migrate to more#
hospitable land, primarily the Nile Valley.#
Hancock's Suggestion: The lost civilization, or a major node of it,#
could have been located in the then-lush Sahara. As it dried, its#
inhabitants brought their advanced knowledge to predynastic Egypt,#
suddenly and mysteriously igniting the sophisticated Pharaonic culture#
with its fully formed hieroglyphs, astronomy, and monumental#
architecture. The lack of excavation in the vast, hostile Sahara means#
this potential evidence remains buried.#
The Appeal: "Explaining a Great Deal”#
Hancock's framework oﬀers a seemingly elegant solution to several#
archaeological puzzles:#
1. The "Puzzle of Civilization": Why do multiple, seemingly isolated#
civilizations arise with complex agriculture, astronomy, and pyramid-#
building almost simultaneously after the Ice Age?#
2. The "Knowledge Leap": Why do cultures like ancient Egypt and Sumer#
appear with their symbolic systems and technical skills already highly#
developed, with little evidence of long, slow evolution?#
3. Shared Motifs: It provides a uniﬁed explanation for striking#
architectural and artistic parallels (pyramids, handbags, serpent#
symbolism, precise stonework).#
Crucially, he frames it as a human-centric, terrestrial alternative to#
"ancient alien" theories. The agents are still Homo sapiens, just from#
a forgotten, advanced chapter of our own history, not#
extraterrestrials.#
of 27 29
Implications: A Lost Civilization with Universal Knowledge
The metrological argument of Barbiero, combined with the archaeological evidence
summarized by Hancock, points to the existence of an advanced, pre‑Flood civilization—one
that possessed precise knowledge of Earth’s tropical year, the mean solar day, and the
mathematical convenience of divisibility. Yet the clues embedded in the numbers 108 and 1.08
suggest that this civilization’s understanding extended far beyond terrestrial observation.#
In the second paper of this trilogy, I derived a relation for the radius of a habitable planet from
the Vedic observation that the Sun’s diameter is approximately 108 times the Earth’s diameter
and that the Earth‑Sun distance is about 108 solar diameters. That relation is#
#
where is the radius of the star and is the orbital radius of the habitable zone.
Using the inverse‑square law for stellar luminosity, , one can
compute the expected planetary radius for stars of any luminosity. When this calculation is
performed for stars across the main sequence—from hot O and B stars through the Sun‑like F
and G stars down to cool K and M dwarfs—the result consistently yields a planet radius close
to that of Earth. In the second paper I demonstrated this explicitly for F, G, and K stars; further
work conﬁrms that the same holds for O, B, A, and M main‑sequence stars as well. In other
words, the equation appears to be a universal constraint for a planet orbiting in the habitable
zone, regardless of the star’s spectral type.#
What does this have to do with a lost civilization? The number 108 was preserved in Vedic
traditions and appears repeatedly in ancient sacred architecture (e.g., the 108 statues at
Angkor Wat, the 54 towers and 540 eﬃgies that are multiples of 108). Barbiero’s analysis
shows that the same civilization that divided the day into 80 000 parts to obtain a natural time
unit of 1.08 s must have understood the tropical year to high precision. To also recognize the
108‑based scaling between the Sun, Earth, and Moon—and to encode it in their calendar and
mythology—would have required them to know the Earth’s radius, the Sun’s radius, and the
Earth‑Sun distance with considerable accuracy.#
Yet the universality of the planetary‑radius equation implies something even more remarkable: if
the lost civilization was aware of the 108 relation and understood its signiﬁcance, they would
have recognized that the same scaling holds for *any* star and its habitable planet. That is,
they would have possessed a physical insight that we moderns only formalized after the
development of the inverse‑square law, stellar astronomy, and the concept of the habitable
zone. They would have known that the conditions for a life‑supporting world are not unique to
our Solar System but are governed by a simple, universal law.#
How could such knowledge have been obtained? One possibility is that the civilization was
purely terrestrial but achieved a level of astronomical and physical understanding far beyond
what we conventionally attribute to any culture before the Scientiﬁc Revolution. Alternatively,
the fact that their insights transcend a single star system might suggest a perspective that was
not limited to Earth—a civilization that either originated elsewhere or had direct experience with
other planetary systems. While such speculation goes beyond the archaeological evidence, it is
consistent with the numbers and relations that have survived in our timekeeping and sacred
traditions.#

In either case, the convergence of my physical ﬁndings (the 1‑second invariant and the
universal planetary‑radius equation) with the metrological and archaeological evidence
presented by Barbiero and Hancock strongly supports the idea that a sophisticated precursor
civilization existed—one that deliberately embedded its knowledge in the units and symbols it
passed down to later cultures. The second, as we use it today, may be a faint echo of that
legacy.#
Conclusion
This trilogy of papers began with the discovery of a characteristic time of one second that
appears to be invariant across scales—from the proton’s radius to the dynamics of the
Earth‑Moon‑Sun system. In the ﬁrst paper, a normal force with was
shown to unify the masses and radii of the proton, neutron, and electron, with the golden ratio
and Fibonacci structure emerging naturally. In the second paper, the same one‑second
timescale was found to govern a quantum‑analog description of the Solar System, where the
Moon serves as the metric and the number 108—central to Vedic cosmology—leads to a
general equation for the radius of a habitable planet around any star.#
The present paper has examined the archaeological and metrological roots of the second.
Following the work of Flavio Barbiero, we have seen that the modern second (86 400 per day)
can be understood as a modiﬁcation of a more natural unit obtained by dividing the mean solar
day into 80 000 parts. That natural unit is . The factor 1.08 and its multiple 108 are
found throughout ancient sacred architecture and calendar systems, pointing to a common
source—a lost civilization of great sophistication. The archaeological evidence summarized by
Graham Hancock, including the global appearance of the “handbag” motif and the migration
from the Green Sahara, provides a plausible context for the transmission of this knowledge.#
The most striking implication is that this lost civilization’s knowledge was not limited to Earth’s
sky. The universal planetary‑radius equation derived from the Vedic 108 relation holds for
main‑sequence stars of all spectral types, from O to M. Thus the same numbers that guided
their calendar and timekeeping also encode a physical law that applies throughout the cosmos.
Whether this reﬂects an ancient terrestrial culture that achieved an extraordinary level of
theoretical understanding, or a civilization with extraterrestrial perspectives, remains an open
question. What is clear is that the convergence of modern physics, ancient metrology, and
symbolic archaeology invites us to reconsider the depth of human history—and perhaps the
nature of those who came before.#
